summaries.text: Emil Carlebach, a former Blockältester from Frankfurt a.Main, left behind a powerful ten-page testimony in German capturing the tragic history of Buchenwald's Jewish prisoners from Spring 1938 to liberation day on April 11, 1945. His report documents the agony of camp-to-camp deportation transports, constant ill-treatment, and the mass murder of Jewish lives. As an active member of the Jewish Group within the Antifascist Organisation, Carlebach chronicles the courageous resistance fighters who stood up against the SS, successfully eradicated camp antisemitism, and saved many fellow prisoners. The account also preserves the memory of pre-war Prussian Landtags president Ernst Heilmann and notes the horrors of guard dogs, insanity, bribery, and the suffering of Dutch Jews.

This record honors Kurt Eisner junior (mentioned on page 5) while recalling the grim beginnings of Buchenwald in 1938. When the first 1,000 Jewish prisoners arrived, they faced a double terror: the brutality of the SS and criminal kapos who collaborated with their captors. The testimony names the perpetrators — Camp-commander Koch, Oberscharführer Sommer, and camp doctor Dr. Hoven — alongside lists of SS officials and kapos. Yet, amidst accounts of cruel medical experiments, mutiny, and sabotage, the record preserves stories of hope, documenting names of brave resistance fighters and instances of non-Jews risking everything to help Jewish prisoners across locations like Auschwitz, Mauthausen's Camp Gusen, Magdeburg, and Zeist.

The 1,000 prisoners swept up in the arrest campaigns came from all walks of life, their diverse ages and backgrounds making it difficult to stand united against their persecutors. Huddled together in an empty, unfurnished cowshed, they were starved on just 300 grams of bread and 3/4 litre of watery soup a day, which cruel supervisors often stole from them. Forced to labor 14 to 15 hours daily, they survived on only 5 hours of sleep a night. Under relentless abuse and attacks in the quarry, three or four desperate souls were driven daily to run toward the guard line, only to be shot down under the guise of escape attempts. Except for a few who were released, almost all were murdered.

On September 23 and 24, 1938, Buchenwald, now operating as a liquidation camp, received two new transports of about a thousand Jewish men each. Most were Viennese citizens rounded up following the occupation of Vienna, but among them were long-term prisoners held for their anti-fascist convictions. In the weeks that followed, imprisoned German anti-fascists waged a determined struggle, successfully purging the overseer ranks of criminal elements. This hard-won victory brought vital protection to the Jewish prisoners, culminating in the Kommandant's January 30 decree that Jewish prisoners would finally be supervised by Jewish block leaders.

Jewish prisoners in Buchenwald were treated as complete pariahs, subjected to horrific working hours and neglect that claimed countless lives. On November 9, 1938, in the wake of the Rath campaign, more than 12,000 Jewish men were brought to the camp. The SS lay in wait in the streets of Weimar, armed with whips and clubs, beating the new arrivals so brutally that hardly any reached the camp unhurt. The streets of Weimar resembled a bloody battlefield, littered with torn luggage, bloodied clothes, and the broken bodies of injured men lying where they fell.

Twelve thousand new arrivals were packed up to four deep into five emergency barracks with no toilets, no straw to sleep on, and no blankets. Chased and terrorized by SS guards using whips and pistols, the first night dissolved into absolute chaos and panic. SS guards selected prisoners at random for cruel reprisals, chaining them in circles to be mauled by dogs. Seventy men lost their sanity that night; they were thrown into a wooden shed and later dragged to the cells to be beaten by Oberscharführer Sommer. The SS targeted well-off business owners, exploiting their suffering to extort their wealth.

Corruption ran rampant among the SS, who extorted prisoners down to the lowest ranks. Loudspeakers blared commands promising early release to anyone who handed over their homes or cars, and the guards extorted thousands of Marks daily under fake charges of 'damage' or release fees. By the time the campaign ended in a mass release a few weeks later — after claiming hundreds of lives — millions in extorted money had passed through the camp. Ironically, this financial greed and cooperation with criminal prisoners weakened the SS command, forcing Kommandant Koch to place political prisoners in overseer roles to restore order.

At the outbreak of war in September 1939, the Kommandant cruelly banned all medical care for Jewish prisoners, though brave comrades in the sick bay quietly ignored the order. Soon after, 2,500 stateless Jews from Vienna and the East were brought in, including the entire populations of old age homes and boarding schools. They were kept behind barbed wire in five large tents on the parade ground under the notorious SS-Scharführers Blank and Hinkelmann, who made no secret of their plan to liquidate them to the last man. Camp Leader Hüttig merely shook his head, astonished that these vulnerable people had been allowed to live free for so long.

The SS waged a campaign of annihilation in the tent camp, killing prisoners through grueling labor, shooting them on false escape charges, withholding underwear, cutting food rations in half, and leaving them standing in freezing winter weather until sixty died every day. SS-Scharführers Blank, Hinkelmann, and Sommer took personal delight in the cruelty, even giving out poisoned food. To stop this horror, the anti-fascist underground fought back, successfully replacing the brutal foreman of the food gang with their own comrade Kurt Posener, who risked his life to help.

Risking his life daily, comrade Kurt Posener smuggled food to the starving inmates of the tent camp. While dangerous collaborators Wolf and Rosenbaum remained in power, the underground placed Polish comrade Max Vulkan as clerk, who worked tirelessly to save lives. In early 1940, Walter Krämer, the courageous head of the sick bay who was later murdered in November 1940, convinced the camp doctor to evacuate this disease-ridden sub-camp to protect the main camp and nearby villages. Setting up an illegal ambulance service, the hospital comrades rescued five hundred barely living survivors from that hellhole.

On November 9, 1939, following the beer hall assassination attempt in Munich, the SS locked Buchenwald's Jewish prisoners in darkened barracks. A gang of guards, including Scharführers Blank and Jännisch, dragged 21 young, strong men to the quarry and shot them under the pretext of escape. The rest were locked away without food for three days, a punishment made worse when the entire camp was starved for five days over an allegedly stolen pig. The evacuation of the tent camp did not end the SS's murderous plans, as the survivors were forced into lethal rock-breaking details in the quarry.

In the quarry, under the brutal command of Blank and Hinkelmann, Jewish prisoners were systematically tortured and shot. The camp's organized resistance suffered a heavy blow on May 3, 1940, when Rudi Arndt, a Jewish Berlin anti-fascist and beloved block leader of Block 22, was denounced and murdered in the quarry. Arndt, alongside released camp elder Ernst Frommhold, had led the struggle against the SS. Fourteen days later, Polish clerk Max Vulkan was killed in the same way, shortly after the collaborator Wolf was overheard threatening to kill him if he spoke of the tent camp horrors.

In the summer of 1940, Kommandant Koch, camp leader Schobert, and doctor Florstadt visited the darning station — run by sick prisoners — and sent all the Jewish workers to the quarry. Among those shot on their first day of work was Austrian comrade Hans Kunke. The testimony records how Jewish prisoners were targeted for death through constant abuse, starvation, and deliberate poisoning by camp doctors. Despite the efforts of the anti-fascist underground, Jewish inmates remained the most persecuted group in the camp.

In February 1941, Buchenwald received 400 young Dutch Jews. Because the organized resistance had improved camp conditions enough to block immediate executions, the SS transferred them to Camp Gusen near Mauthausen, where they were murdered to the last man within eight days. That year, the camp witnessed a rare act of courage: when Oberscharführer Abraham drowned prisoner Hamber in a puddle, Hamber's brother spoke the truth to inspectors. In retaliation, Oberscharführer Petrik forced the work foreman to add his own name to a list of 28 workers marked for death.

Knowing he was signing his own death warrant, Hamber's brother told the narrator: 'I know that I must die because of what I have said, but perhaps these criminals might hold back a little in future if they are afraid of being reported, and then I will not have died in vain.' Interrogated by Koch and his officers, who gave their 'word of honour' that he would not be harmed, the brother bravely repeated his testimony. He returned to his block at 9:30, but was dragged to the cell block at 11:30. The record ends as he goes to his death.

The cell block was a place of no return; until 1942, no Jewish prisoner ever left it alive. The 29 Jewish workers from Hamber's work group were taken to the cells one by one, each dying on their fourth day of confinement. The camp administration tried to hide this mass murder by transferring the next victims to different work details a day or two beforehand. One prisoner, lying in the hospital with a broken leg, was carried to the cells on a stretcher on Sommer's orders. A short shoemaker named Löwitus, aged about 40, was saved only because he obtained permission to emigrate to the Soviet Union.

This record honors the memory of Jochen Pickard, a Rhineland anti-fascist who had survived Dachau. He had confessed to political crimes to get transferred to a penitentiary, but upon finishing his sentence, he was sent to Buchenwald. The SS, remembering his years of defiance, sent him to the cell block immediately, where he was killed. It also remembers Ernst Heilmann, the Social Democratic Party President of the Prussian Parliament, who was thrown into the cell block in 1940 for no reason at all and died shortly after.

Kurt Eisner, son of the former Bavarian Minister-President, had survived multiple assassination attempts but was finally killed in the cells after refusing to betray a comrade, telling the Lagerführer: 'I have been a prisoner for ten years. You will not expect me to denounce a comrade.' In the winter of 1941, the SS began typhus experiments on Jewish prisoners in groups of ten and fifty, stopping only because of Nazi racial laws against injecting Jewish blood serum into Aryans. In early 1942, Dr. Hoven sent 400 Jews declared unfit for work on 'invalid transports' to a secret poison gas station.

A year after the invalid transports, the records office was ordered to register the 400 victims as deceased. Dr. Hoven threatened prisoner Weingärtner of Mannheim with hanging if the truth got out, unaware that Weingärtner had falsified the lists to save many comrades. The narrator stresses that their core mission was protecting their cleanest, most dedicated resistance cadres — a strategy proven right when the camp was liberated on April 11, 1945. The transports were carried out over four days, with thirty men loaded onto three trucks at morning parade each day.

On the final day of the transports, sick prisoners were thrown into the trucks wearing only shirts. On October 17, 1942, the SS deported all remaining Jewish prisoners to Auschwitz, leaving only 200 skilled armaments workers behind, including the narrator. Their position improved as the organized underground successfully fought antisemitism among the prisoners, and the SS spared them for their labor. Yet, in the summer of 1943, a new crisis arose with the arrival of Scharführers Schmidt and Greuel, Simons, and Hofschulte.
